steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Ticket #4891 — user says their static site at /home/user/www isn't accessible. They've got index.html and some assets in there, just need a basic http server on port 8080. Python's already installed, don't need anything fancy, just get it serving so they can hit localhost:8080 and see the page.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@65b7645b0b6b:/home/user#

Analysis: I'm currently in /home/user directory as root. I need to check if the /home/user/www directory exists, verify it contains index.html and assets, and then start a simple Python HTTP server on port 8080 to serve the static content.
Plan: First, I'll list the contents of /home/user to see if the www directory exists. Then I'll navigate to the www directory and check its contents. Finally, I'll start Python's built-in HTTP server on port 8080 to serve the static files.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

New Terminal Output:

root@65b7645b0b6b:/home/user# ls -la
total 12
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 08:52 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 08:52 ..
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 08:52 www
root@65b7645b0b6b:/home/user#

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! The www directory exists in /home/user. Now I need to check its contents to verify that index.html and assets are present, then start the Python HTTP server.
Plan: I'll cd into the www directory, list its contents to confirm index.html and assets are there, then start Python's HTTP server on port 8080 using 'python3 -m http.server 8080'.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@65b7645b0b6b:/home/user# cd www
root@65b7645b0b6b:/home/user/www# ls -la
total 16
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 08:52 .
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 08:52 ..
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 08:52 css
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  332 Apr 29 08:52 index.html
root@65b7645b0b6b:/home/user/www#

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! I can see the www directory contains index.html and a css directory (assets). Now I need to start the Python HTTP server on port 8080 to make the site accessible.
Plan: I'll start Python's built-in HTTP server on port 8080 using 'python3 -m http.server 8080'. This will serve the static files from the current directory (/home/user/www) and make them accessible at localhost:8080.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@65b7645b0b6b:/home/user/www# python3 -m http.server 8080
Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8080 (http://0.0.0.0:8080/) ...

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! The Python HTTP server is now running successfully on port 8080. The server is serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0:8080, which means it's accessible on all network interfaces including localhost:8080. The static site at /home/user/www is now accessible, and users can visit localhost:8080 to see the index.html page and its assets.
Plan: The task is complete. The HTTP server is running and serving the static site. The user can now access their site at localhost:8080.

Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.

steps.message:
Analysis: The Python HTTP server is running successfully on port 8080, serving the static site from /home/user/www. The server message 'Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8080' confirms it's active and listening. The site with index.html and css assets is now accessible at localhost:8080, which fulfills all the requirements from ticket #4891.
Plan: The task is complete. The basic HTTP server is running on port 8080 as requested, serving the static site files.
